title: Judo Match Video Analyzer
description: A scrappy tool to tag exchanges and track what actually worked under pressure.
story: I started this because my memory of a match was always emotional and selective. The first time I plotted when I got thrown, I realized I was losing the same grip battle every time. It wasn’t flattering, but it was useful. The lesson: footage doesn’t care about your excuses, and that’s exactly why it helps.

title: Deal Memo Generator
description: A template-driven memo builder so I could spend time thinking, not formatting.
story: I used to waste energy polishing the same sections over and over. Once I turned the structure into reusable blocks, the writing got sharper because I could focus on the argument. Weirdly, constraints made it easier to be honest: if I couldn’t state the risk in one sentence, I didn’t understand it yet.

title: Cap Rate Curve Sketchbook
description: A small sandbox for stress-testing cap rate shifts across asset types.
story: I built this after one too many conversations where people argued about ‘the’ cap rate as if it was a single number. When I forced myself to draw curves (not points), the uncertainty became visible. It made me calmer in meetings because I could say, ‘here’s the sensitivity,’ instead of pretending I knew the future.

title: Retail Footfall Heatmap
description: A prototype to compare pedestrian intensity by block and time of day.
story: I thought I was looking for the ‘best’ corner. What I actually found was how quickly the story changes after 6pm. The first version of the map was flashy but misleading—too many colors, too little meaning. The second version was quieter, and finally told the truth.

title: Recipe Cost Optimizer
description: A simple calculator for price-per-protein and pantry planning.
story: This one was personal: I wanted to eat well without turning food into a second job. Once I tracked cost-per-meal, I stopped ‘optimizing’ the wrong thing. The bigger insight wasn’t about money—it was about removing friction so I’d actually follow through.

title: Zoning Change Tracker
description: A running log of policy changes with what they actually changed on the ground.
story: I used to read zoning updates like they were abstract text. Then I started translating each change into: what becomes legal, what gets harder, and who benefits. The exercise made me more skeptical of neutral language. Small words can move millions of dollars.

title: Land Value Capture Case Study
description: A structured comparison of value capture tools and political tradeoffs.
story: I kept seeing the same debate: ‘developers should pay more’ versus ‘we need housing.’ Once I laid out the mechanisms side by side, the argument got more concrete. The uncomfortable part is that the ‘best’ policy depends on who you’re willing to disappoint.

title: Portfolio Underwriting Sandbox
description: A lightweight underwriting model to compare deals on the same footing.
story: I built this because I didn’t trust my own instincts when the numbers were presented differently each time. Standardizing the inputs made patterns show up immediately—especially where assumptions were doing all the work. It became a credibility check: if a deal only works with heroic assumptions, call it that.

title: Real Estate Carbon Budget Dashboard
description: A simple view comparing operational vs embodied emissions across scenarios.
story: The first draft made it look like there was a single ‘green’ answer. Then I separated embodied from operational carbon, and the tradeoffs stopped hiding. The result was messier, but more honest. That felt like real progress: the dashboard didn’t persuade, it clarified.

title: Transit Catchment Story Map
description: A narrative map showing walk sheds and transfer friction around key stations.
story: I assumed the map would be about distance. It ended up being about time and patience. Two stops that look close can feel far if the transfer is miserable. Making that visible changed how I thought about ‘access’—it’s not geometry, it’s lived experience.

title: Housing Affordability Explorer
description: Small multiples for rent burden and income bands, with neighborhood filters.
story: I wanted a chart that didn’t shame people for struggling. When I got the bins wrong, the entire story tilted toward the wealthy neighborhoods. Fixing it wasn’t just ‘data cleaning’—it was an ethical correction. The final version is still imperfect, but at least it doesn’t lie by accident.

title: Campus Mobility Map
description: A quick explorable map prototype for commuting patterns around Cambridge.
story: This started as a fun mapping exercise and turned into a lesson in constraint. The best interactions weren’t the fancy ones—they were the ones that answered a single question quickly. After a few iterations, I realized my job wasn’t to show everything. It was to help someone decide what to do next.
